summary: The OwlyGPT content calendar generation screen suffers from multiple typographical errors that undermine credibility, a table rendered as an image of text which creates significant accessibility barriers, and unclear interaction patterns in the input area. While the overall layout is clean and the sidebar navigation is well-organized, the AI-generated content quality and presentation need substantial improvement.

issues.title: Content calendar is rendered as an image of text
issues.location: Main content area — Content Calendar table for March 2026
issues.problem: The entire content calendar table appears to be rendered as an image rather than semantic HTML. Screen readers cannot parse the table structure, rows, columns, or cell content. Users cannot select, copy, or interact with individual cell text.
issues.critical_reason: Images of text violate WCAG 1.4.5 because users cannot resize, reflow, or customize the text presentation, and assistive technologies cannot access the content programmatically.
issues.recommendation: Render the calendar as a semantic HTML <table> with proper <th> headers, <tr> rows, and <td> cells. Include a <caption> element describing the table. This also enables responsive behavior on smaller screens.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.4.5 Images of Text (AA): text should be used to convey information rather than images of text

issues.title: Multiple spelling errors in AI-generated calendar content
issues.problem: The calendar contains at least five typographical errors: 'Photd' (Photo), 'Behind Ive scener' (Behind the scenes), 'Tentmonial' (Testimonial), 'Tips and Tucks' (Tips and Tricks), and 'Triday' (Friday). These errors severely undermine trust in the AI tool and the product's professionalism.
issues.critical_reason: Per the aesthetic-usability effect, users perceive sloppy outputs as less trustworthy and functional. Errors in an AI content tool directly contradict the product's core value proposition, causing users to question its reliability.
issues.recommendation: Implement spell-checking and content validation on AI-generated outputs before rendering. Display a disclaimer or allow inline editing of generated content. Consider a review step before finalizing the calendar.
issues.reference: Universal Principles of Design — Aesthetic-Usability Effect: beautiful things are perceived as easier to use

issues.title: Download and thumbs-up icon buttons lack accessible labels
issues.location: Below the calendar table — download icon and thumbs-up icon
issues.problem: The two icon-only buttons beneath the calendar (download and like/feedback) have no visible text labels or apparent aria-labels. Screen reader users cannot determine the purpose of these interactive controls.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 1.1.1 requires all non-text content that is functional to have a text alternative. Icon-only buttons without labels force users to guess functionality, increasing the gulf of execution.
issues.recommendation: Add aria-label attributes such as 'Download calendar' and 'Rate this response' to each button. Consider adding visible tooltip text on hover/focus to aid sighted users as well.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.1.1 Non-text Content (A): all non-text content must have a text alternative

issues.title: Tag 'salmobbin' appears to be a misspelling or unclear label
issues.problem: The tag 'salmobbin' in the input field appears to be a misspelled or unclear label that provides no recognizable meaning to the user. Users cannot understand what this tag represents or why it was applied, creating confusion about the input context.
issues.critical_reason: Per Norman's signifier principle, visible cues must clearly indicate their meaning and purpose. An unrecognizable label increases cognitive load and forces users to investigate or guess its function.
issues.recommendation: Ensure tag labels are human-readable and meaningful (e.g., a brand name or content category). If auto-generated, validate against known terms. Provide a tooltip explaining the tag's purpose on hover.
issues.reference: The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) — Signifier: visible cues must indicate where to act

issues.title: Insufficient contrast on table header text against white background
issues.location: Content Calendar table header row — 'Monday', 'Tuesday', etc.
issues.problem: The bold header text in the table uses a dark color on a white/light gray background, but some cell text within the table body may not meet the 4.5:1 minimum contrast ratio, especially the lighter-weight body text. The overall table rendering as an image also prevents zoom-based contrast improvements.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 1.4.3 requires a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for normal text. Insufficient contrast impacts users with low vision and those viewing screens in bright environments.
issues.recommendation: Ensure all table text meets a minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio against its background. Convert the image-based table to HTML text so users can apply custom styles or zoom without degradation.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum) (AA): normal text requires 4.5:1 contrast ratio

issues.title: No visible focus indicators on interactive elements
issues.location: Sidebar navigation items, input field, icon buttons
issues.problem: There are no visible focus indicators on any interactive elements in the screenshot. Keyboard users navigating through the sidebar, input field, or action buttons would have no visual indication of which element is currently focused.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 2.4.7 requires that keyboard focus indicators are visible. Without them, keyboard-only users cannot orient themselves on the page, making the interface effectively unusable without a mouse.
issues.recommendation: Add a visible focus ring (e.g., 2px solid blue outline with offset) to all focusable elements. Ensure the focus indicator has at least a 3:1 contrast ratio against adjacent colors per WCAG 2.4.13.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 2.4.7 Focus Visible (AA): keyboard focus indicator must be visible

issues.title: Calendar lacks any editing or interaction affordances
issues.problem: The generated calendar appears to be a static image with no way for users to edit individual cells, rearrange content types, or customize the output. Users must regenerate the entire calendar to make any changes, creating an inefficient workflow.
issues.critical_reason: Per Norman's gulf of execution, users who want to tweak one cell cannot figure out how to do so. This forces a costly regeneration cycle and violates the principle of user control and freedom (Nielsen's Heuristic #3).
issues.recommendation: Make the calendar an editable table where users can click cells to modify content types. Add drag-and-drop functionality for rearranging items. Provide an 'Edit' button that converts the static view into an editable mode.
issues.reference: The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) — Gulf of Execution: user cannot figure out how to do what they want

issues.title: Input placeholder text disappears without persistent label
issues.location: Chat input field — 'Describe the image you want to create'
issues.problem: The placeholder text 'Describe the image you want to create' serves as the only label for the input field. Once a user begins typing, this instructional text disappears, leaving no persistent label to remind users of the field's purpose. Additionally, the placeholder says 'image' but the feature generates content calendars, creating a mismatch.
issues.critical_reason: Placeholder-only labels violate WCAG 1.3.1 because the programmatic relationship between label and input is lost. The label/purpose mismatch also increases cognitive dissonance and user confusion.
issues.recommendation: Add a persistent visible label above the input field (e.g., 'Ask OwlyGPT'). Update the placeholder to accurately reflect the tool's capabilities: 'Describe what you want to create — calendars, posts, images, and more.'
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.3.1 Info and Relationships (A): information and relationships conveyed visually must be programmatically determinable

issues.title: Sidebar active state relies primarily on color/fill change
issues.location: Left sidebar — OwlyGPT nav item (highlighted with dark background)
issues.problem: The active navigation item (OwlyGPT) is indicated primarily through a filled dark background circle, while inactive items use outline-style icons. The differentiation is subtle and may not be perceivable by users with color vision deficiencies or low vision.
issues.critical_reason: Per WCAG 1.4.1, color should not be the sole means of conveying information. A subtle fill change may be missed, leaving users uncertain about their current location in the app.
issues.recommendation: Add a secondary indicator such as a left border accent bar, bold text label, or distinct shape change to reinforce the active state. Ensure at least 3:1 contrast between active and inactive states.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.4.1 Use of Color (A): color is not the only visual means of conveying information

issues.title: No confirmation or success feedback after download action
issues.location: Download icon button below the calendar
issues.problem: The download button provides no visible indication of what will happen when clicked (file format, destination) and likely provides no confirmation feedback after the action is completed. Users cannot tell what they're downloading or if it succeeded.
issues.critical_reason: Norman's feedback principle states every action must produce an immediate, clear response. Without feedback, users may click repeatedly or assume the action failed, eroding confidence in the tool.
issues.recommendation: Add a tooltip showing 'Download as PNG' (or relevant format) on hover. After clicking, display a brief toast notification confirming 'Calendar downloaded successfully.' Consider offering format options (PNG, CSV, PDF).
issues.reference: The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) — Feedback: every action must produce an immediate, clear response
